explanation:
**Technical Calculators** for IT: latency, storage, uptime SLA, cloud costs—decisions that scale.

## How It Works

This calculator compares algorithm complexities O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), and O(2^n)—free and instant. It applies the appropriate formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting when numbers fall outside reasonable bounds.

## Important Notes

This calculator provides reference estimates only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a specialized professional. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.a: O(1)—constant time. The operation takes the same number of steps regardless of input size.

faq.q: What's the difference between O(n) and O(n²)?
faq.a: O(n) scales linearly; O(n²) scales quadratically. Double the input: linear doubles execution time, quadratic quadruples it.
Logarithmic complexity scales very well. Doubling input size adds only one operation—like binary search.
Exponential is dangerous. At n=30, you're already at 1 billion operations. Impractical for large inputs.

faq.q: What's the difference between O, Ω, and Θ notation?
faq.a: O = worst-case, Ω = best-case, Θ = average case. Most developers focus on big-O worst-case analysis.
Yes, all our calculators are free and require no sign-up or registration.

faq.q: How accurate are these time estimates?
faq.a: These are reference estimates using standard formulas. Actual times vary by hardware and implementation. For critical performance needs, profile your actual code.

faq.q: Do you store my calculation data?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send data to any server.
